title: Differentially Private Fine-tuning of Language Models

structured.summary: The paper proposes a framework for differentially private fine-tuning of language models focused on parameter efficiency, achieving state-of-the-art privacy-utility trade-offs across several NLP tasks. It utilizes methods like DPSGD and reparametrized gradient perturbation to improve computational efficiency and maintain high accuracy.

strengths.evidence: The paper achieves high accuracy on MNLI and E2E datasets under differential privacy constraints, closely matching non-private models (Section on 'OUR RESULTS').
The framework updates only a small fraction of the model's parameters, reducing computational and memory costs (Section on 'OUR APPROACH').
The modular design allows adaptation for various downstream tasks with minimal overhead (Section on 'OUR CONTRIBUTIONS').

weaknesses.evidence: Lacks clear delineation of novelty compared to existing parameter-efficient techniques, primarily leveraging DPSGD and RGP (Section on 'OUR CONTRIBUTIONS').
Lacks specific details about the hardware used, hyperparameter settings, and fine-tuning datasets size (Section on 'OUR APPROACH').

weaknesses.point: Lack of clarity in mechanism evaluation.
weaknesses.evidence: Doesn't clearly explain how parameter-efficient techniques interact with DP mechanisms (Sections on 'REPARAMETRIZED GRADIENT PERTURBATION' and 'OUR APPROACH').
Limited to comparisons primarily with previous work (Yu et al., 2021b), without broader validation (Section on 'State-of-the-art utility and privacy').
No substantial discussion on limitations or potential downsides of the approach (General absence in sections on results and approaches).
Claims efficiency without detailing metrics or evaluation criteria (Section on 'OUR CONTRIBUTIONS').
Evaluation focuses on MNLI and E2E datasets, lacking broader demonstration of applicability (Section on 'Empirical evaluation').
Privacy budgets ε=6.7, δ=1e-6 are used without justification related to real-world privacy (Section on 'State-of-the-art utility and privacy').
Claims larger models are always better under DP without covering varying tasks or models (Section on 'Larger models are better').
Focuses on empirical results without theoretical models or analysis on the framework's behavior (Absence noted throughout methodological descriptions).

questions.question: How can parameter efficiency be quantitatively evaluated within this framework?
questions.motivation: Clear metrics are essential to validate efficiency claims and assess performance improvements.

questions.question: What are the potential real-world implications of the chosen privacy budgets (ε, δ)?
questions.motivation: Understanding the practical implications and justifying these values is important for privacy assurances.

questions.question: Are there scenarios where smaller models could outperform larger models under differential privacy settings?
questions.motivation: Identifying contexts where smaller models excel can guide practical model selection decisions in resource-constrained environments.

critique_points.point_001: Ambiguity in novelty and contribution.. Lacks clear delineation of novelty compared to existing parameter-efficient techniques, primarily leveraging DPSGD and RGP (Section on 'OUR CONTRIBUTIONS')
critique_points.point_002: Insufficient experimental details.. Lacks specific details about the hardware used, hyperparameter settings, and fine-tuning datasets size (Section on 'OUR APPROACH')
critique_points.point_003: Lack of clarity in mechanism evaluation.. Doesn't clearly explain how parameter-efficient techniques interact with DP mechanisms (Sections on 'REPARAMETRIZED GRADIENT PERTURBATION' and 'OUR APPROACH')
critique_points.point_004: Overstated claims on utility.. Limited to comparisons primarily with previous work (Yu et al., 2021b), without broader validation (Section on 'State-of-the-art utility and privacy')
critique_points.point_005: Inadequate discussion on limitations.. No substantial discussion on limitations or potential downsides of the approach (General absence in sections on results and approaches)
critique_points.point_006: Vague description of parameter efficiency.. Claims efficiency without detailing metrics or evaluation criteria (Section on 'OUR CONTRIBUTIONS')
critique_points.point_007: Limited dataset range for evaluation.. Evaluation focuses on MNLI and E2E datasets, lacking broader demonstration of applicability (Section on 'Empirical evaluation')
critique_points.point_008: Inconsistencies in privacy budget justifications.. Privacy budgets ε=6.7, δ=1e-6 are used without justification related to real-world privacy (Section on 'State-of-the-art utility and privacy')
critique_points.point_009: Misleading generalization about larger models.. Claims larger models are always better under DP without covering varying tasks or models (Section on 'Larger models are better')
critique_points.point_010: Minimal theoretical analysis provided.. Focuses on empirical results without theoretical models or analysis on the framework's behavior (Absence noted throughout methodological descriptions)
